content:
# Chapter 4: Picking Your AI Model

*In which we compare the major models so you don't have to read 47 benchmarks.*

## The Model Landscape

When people say "use AI in your app," they usually mean: call an API provided by one of the major AI labs, which runs a large language model on their servers and returns a response.

The major players as of this writing are:

- **OpenAI** — GPT-4o, GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-3.5 Turbo, o1
- **Anthropic** — Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Claude 3 Opus, Claude 3 Haiku
- **Google** — Gemini 1.5 Pro, Gemini 1.5 Flash, Gemini 1.0
- **Meta** — Llama 3 (open source, run yourself or via third-party hosts)
- **Mistral** — Mistral Large, Mistral 7B (also open source)
- **Cohere** — Command R+ (optimized for enterprise/RAG use cases)

That's a lot. Here's the good news: they all work roughly the same way from a developer standpoint. You send a prompt, you get a response. The API structure is similar enough that switching between them takes maybe an hour of code changes.

So don't get too attached to any one model. Pick one to start, learn the pattern, and know that the skills transfer.

---

## How to Think About Model Selection

Forget benchmark scores for a minute. Here's what actually matters when picking a model for an app:

### 1. Capability for Your Task

Different models have different strengths. For heavy reasoning and nuanced writing: Claude Opus or GPT-4. For coding: GPT-4o or Claude 3.5 Sonnet are both excellent. For speed and cost on simpler tasks: GPT-3.5 Turbo, Claude Haiku, or Gemini Flash.

The best way to evaluate: run your actual prompts through multiple models and compare outputs. Don't trust benchmarks you didn't run yourself on your actual use case.

### 2. Cost

Models charge per token (input + output). Prices vary enormously:

| Model (approximate) | Input (per 1M tokens) | Output (per 1M tokens) |
|---|---|---|
| GPT-4o | ~$5 | ~$15 |
| GPT-3.5 Turbo | ~$0.50 | ~$1.50 |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet | ~$3 | ~$15 |
| Claude 3 Haiku | ~$0.25 | ~$1.25 |
| Gemini 1.5 Flash | ~$0.075 | ~$0.30 |

*(Prices change frequently — always check the provider's current pricing page.)*

A simple rule: use the cheapest model that gives good enough output for your use case. Don't use a $15/M-token model for tasks where a $1/M-token model does the job.

### 3. Speed / Latency

For user-facing apps where someone is waiting for a response, latency matters. Smaller, faster models (Haiku, Flash, GPT-3.5 Turbo) return responses in 1–3 seconds. Larger models can take 10–30 seconds for long outputs.

**Streaming** is your friend here: most APIs support streaming the response token by token, so the user sees words appearing immediately rather than waiting for the full response. We'll implement this in Chapter 10.

### 4. Context Window

If your app needs to process long documents or maintain long conversations, you need a large context window. Gemini 1.5 Pro has a 1M token context window — you can feed it an entire book. GPT-4 Turbo has 128K. Claude has up to 200K.

### 5. Rate Limits

Each model has rate limits — maximum requests per minute, tokens per minute, tokens per day. For a small project, this rarely matters. For a production app with many users, you need to understand these limits and potentially use multiple API keys or providers.

### 6. Data Privacy

What happens to the data you send? OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google all have enterprise tiers with stronger data privacy guarantees (no training on your data, etc.). For apps handling sensitive information, check the provider's data usage policies before building.

> ⚠️ **Watch Out!**
> If you're building for healthcare, legal, or finance, pay close attention to data privacy. Sending patient records or legal documents to an AI API without reviewing the data processing agreements could create serious compliance problems.

---

## My Recommendation for Beginners

Start with **OpenAI's GPT-4o** or **Anthropic's Claude 3.5 Sonnet**. Both have:
- Excellent documentation
- Large developer communities (lots of tutorials, Stack Overflow answers, examples)
- Free tiers or low-cost tiers for experimentation
- Well-designed APIs

For budget development and prototyping, use **GPT-3.5 Turbo** or **Claude Haiku**. They're 10-20x cheaper and fast. Once you've validated your approach, you can upgrade to more capable models for production.

> 🐇 **Bunny Tip**
> OpenAI has a Playground (platform.openai.com/playground) where you can experiment with different models and settings without writing any code. Use it to prototype your prompts before building them into an app. Claude has a similar tool at console.anthropic.com.

---

## Open Source Models: Llama and Friends

The models mentioned above are proprietary — you pay to use them via API. There's another world: open source models that you can run yourself.

**Meta's Llama 3** (and its fine-tuned variants) is the most prominent. You can run Llama:
- Locally on your computer (with sufficient RAM/VRAM) using tools like Ollama or LM Studio
- Via third-party hosting services like Together AI, Replicate, or Groq
- On your own cloud server

Why would you run open source?
- **Cost:** Pay for compute, not per-token fees. Cheaper at scale.
- **Privacy:** Data never leaves your infrastructure.
- **Customization:** Fine-tune on your own data more easily.
- **No rate limits:** As much as your hardware can handle.

Why might you not?
- **Complexity:** More infrastructure to manage.
- **Capability gap:** The best open source models are still behind the frontier proprietary models for complex tasks (though the gap is closing fast).
- **Latency:** Running locally on consumer hardware is slow.

For beginners: start with a hosted API. Run open source models later when you have a specific reason to.

---

## The API Abstraction Layer

One more tool worth knowing: there are libraries and services that wrap multiple AI APIs behind a single interface.

**LangChain** and **LlamaIndex** (Python) let you swap between different model providers with minimal code changes, and add a lot of useful utilities for building AI apps (prompt templates, conversation memory, document retrieval).

**Vercel AI SDK** (JavaScript/TypeScript) does the same thing and integrates well with web development.

These are useful tools but also add abstraction. For learning, I'd recommend using the model's native API directly first — understand the raw mechanism — and then adopt a framework once you know what it's doing for you.

> 🔨 **Try It Yourself**
> Sign up for an OpenAI account at platform.openai.com. Don't put in a credit card yet — explore the Playground with the free tier. Send a few prompts. Look at the token count for each request/response. Notice what different temperature settings do. Get comfortable with the interface before we write any code.

---

## Summary

- Major model providers: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta (open source), Mistral (open source)
- Evaluate models on: capability for your task, cost, speed, context window, rate limits, data privacy
- Start with GPT-4o or Claude 3.5 Sonnet; use cheaper/faster models for prototyping
- Open source models exist and are worth knowing about, but start with hosted APIs
- All the major models work similarly from a developer standpoint — the skills transfer
